result.question:
Final dedicated research round on ONE compound area that two previous rounds crowded out of verification. EVERY claim extracted and verified in this round must concern goal elicitation methods or the agent-scaffold prior-art landscape — no context-engineering, memory, enforcement, or telemetry claims. This answers two design decisions for a Claude Code plugin ("kernel + builder" that interviews a user and scaffolds a personalized, long-lived agentic system with metrics, local telemetry, and a guarded self-improvement loop). Prefer primary sources; flag thin evidence. Model tiering: cheap models (haiku) for search/fetch/verify; strongest model for synthesis only.

SUB-AREA 1 — GOAL/REQUIREMENTS ELICITATION (decision: how the builder's setup interview should work):
- Requirements-engineering evidence comparing elicitation techniques: structured vs unstructured interviews, questionnaires, prototyping, scenarios (Davis et al. 2006 systematic review "Effectiveness of Requirements Elicitation Techniques"; Dieste & Juristo 2011 aggregation). Which techniques elicit more/better requirements?
- Goal-setting theory: Locke & Latham — specific, difficult goals outperform vague/"do your best" goals; moderators (commitment, feedback, task complexity).
- Implementation intentions: Gollwitzer & Sheeran meta-analysis — if-then plans, effect size (d ≈ .65) on goal attainment.
- Jobs-to-be-Done / switch interviews (Christensen, Moesta): documented method structure and any evidence of effectiveness.
- Preference elicitation and preference drift in personalized/recommender systems: cold-start interview design, how systems detect and adapt to drifting user preferences over time.

SUB-AREA 2 — PRIOR ART LANDSCAPE (decision: is the niche filled; what to learn/steal from public reception):
Catalog each of these with what it scaffolds and whether it includes (1) interview/onboarding, (2) per-system success metrics, (3) usage telemetry, (4) self-review/self-improvement loop:
- Agent OS by Builder Methods (buildermethods.com/agent-os) — spec-driven agentic development standards
- SuperClaude framework (GitHub) and claude-flow (ruvnet)
- awesome-claude-code ecosystem; Anthropic official Claude Code plugins + marketplace (anthropics/claude-code docs, plugin announcement Oct 2025)
- AGENTS.md standard (agents.md) adoption
- Letta (formerly MemGPT) product; Mem0 (mem0ai); Zep / Graphiti memory layers
- ChatGPT memory features (OpenAI docs); Claude memory feature
- PKM-agent hybrids: Obsidian Copilot / Smart Connections, Khoj, Rewind/Limitless
- Public reception where findable: GitHub star counts and issue themes, Hacker News threads, r/ClaudeAI discussions — what do users praise and complain about in these scaffolds (e.g., token bloat from big frameworks, abandoned configs, complexity)?
The competitive question: does ANY existing public tool combine interview-driven personalization + scaffolded runtime discipline + local usage telemetry + a guarded self-improvement loop in one package, or is that combination genuinely unfilled? If unfilled, which existing tools come closest and on which dimensions?
result.summary: For the builder's setup interview, the evidence favors a structured, artifact-driven elicitation over open-ended questioning: a peer-reviewed family of experiments (167 subjects) found Paper Prototyping elicits the most requirements while unstructured interviews are fast but low-quality and redundant — so the builder should interview around concrete draft scaffolds, not free-form chat. Critically, a preregistered N=470 experiment shows LLM-authored goals score far higher on SMART criteria yet gut psychological ownership and real follow-through (72.8% vs 46.6% acted on goals at two weeks), so the user must author their own goals with the builder refining, and the goal-setting literature (implementation intentions d=.65 overall, d=.61 for getting started; MCII g=0.24-0.34, stronger when delivered interactively) says the interview should convert goals into if-then plans through dialogue rather than generated documents. On prior art, the verified landscape shows the combination is unfilled: individual pieces exist in the Claude Code ecosystem — a 6-question setup interview (mobile-spine), local offline telemetry (CCUsage), independent-model self-review (Bouncer, Review-squad), and an ungated self-improvement loop (SuperClaude's PM Agent) — but no verified tool combines interview-driven personalization, runtime discipline, local telemetry, and a guarded self-improvement loop; notably SuperClaude, the closest large framework, has no onboarding interview at all and its self-improvement loop lacks any human gate. Coverage of the broader prior-art list (Agent OS, claude-flow, Letta/Mem0/Zep, PKM hybrids, public reception data) did not survive verification this round, so the "unfilled niche" conclusion is strong for the Claude Code plugin ecosystem specifically but only presumptive beyond it.

findings.claim: Structured, artifact-based elicitation beats unstructured interviewing: Paper Prototyping elicits significantly more requirements than JAD or Unstructured Interviews and is the method of choice for functional requirements; JAD is slowest but best for non-functional requirements and diversity (lowest overlap); Unstructured Interviews are fastest but elicit the fewest requirements with the most redundancy, recommended only when speed matters more than quality.
findings.evidence: Rueda, Panach & Distante 2020 (Information and Software Technology), family of 4 experiments over 3 years, 167 subjects. Verbatim results: 'Paper Prototyping is the method that... identifies the highest number of requirements; JAD is the slowest method... and the method with the best diversity; Unstructured Interviews is the fastest method, the method that elicits fewer requirements and the method with more overlapping.' Conclusions section maps each method to its niche. Design implication: the builder interview should present concrete draft scaffolds/prototypes for reaction rather than asking open-ended questions. Caveats: student subjects, no industrial validation, some quality metrics non-significant (p=0.59).

findings.claim: Users must author their own goals: LLM-authored personal goals score dramatically higher on SMART criteria (d=2.26) but produce substantially lower psychological ownership (d=1.38), commitment (d=1.19), and perceived importance (d=1.13), and at two-week follow-up only 46.6% of LLM-condition participants had acted on 2+ goals versus 72.8% of self-authors.
findings.evidence: Chi et al. 2026, 'Optimized but Unowned', preregistered (OSF) between-subjects experiment, N=470 (332 at follow-up), verified verbatim against the preprint across three fetches. Well-formed goals are not owned goals. Design implication: the builder must have the user articulate goals in their own words and only refine/structure them, never generate them wholesale. Confidence capped at medium because it is a single not-yet-peer-reviewed preprint; the claimed mediation mechanism (psychological ownership) was separately REFUTED 0-3, so cite outcomes, not mechanism.

findings.claim: Converting elicited goals into if-then implementation intentions has robust meta-analytic support: overall d=.65 across 94 studies (Gollwitzer & Sheeran 2006), and d=.61 specifically for overcoming failures to get started (remembering to act, missing opportunities, overcoming initial reluctance).
findings.evidence: Both figures corroborated across multiple independent sources including an author-written chapter and citation in the 2021 MCII meta-analysis; the exact abstract text was matched but the original 2006 primary PDF was not directly read (ScienceDirect 403), hence medium rather than high. Design implication: the builder interview's output format should be if-then plans ('when X happens, the system does / I do Y'), which is especially effective for the get-started problems a long-lived agentic system targets.

findings.claim: Mental Contrasting with Implementation Intentions (MCII) works but modestly, and delivery format matters: overall g=0.336 (24 effect sizes, N=15,907), dropping to g=0.242 after trim-and-fill publication-bias correction; interactive experimenter-delivered interventions significantly outperform document/written delivery (g=0.465 vs 0.277, p<.05).
findings.evidence: Wang, Wang & Gai 2021, Frontiers in Psychology meta-analysis, all figures verified verbatim against the primary source. The delivery moderator is directly actionable: a conversational interview that interactively quality-controls the user's plans should beat handing the user a generated planning document — supporting a dialogue-based builder over a template-fill onboarding. Caveat: adjusted and unadjusted CIs overlap, so treat the bias-corrected drop as a point-estimate shift, not a significant difference.

findings.claim: Cold-start preference elicitation design has two verified technical lessons: (1) pairwise comparisons outperform single-item questions, needing fewer user interactions for comparable recommendation quality; (2) selection bias introduced during the elicitation stage compounds downstream — it exacerbates overrepresentation of popular items in later recommendations, was unstudied before 2023 despite elicitation data being especially sparse and bias-prone, and applying IPS debiasing to the elicitation loss significantly improves downstream error metrics (though ranking-metric gains are mixed).
findings.evidence: Pairwise finding verified verbatim in the Oct 2025 preprint (Yahoo! Music data, 1,701 users): pairwise elicitation 'provides more informative signals... thereby requiring fewer RE requests from users.' Selection-bias findings verified verbatim in Gupta, Oosterhuis & de Rijke (CONSEQUENCES @ RecSys'23): simulation-based, IPS beats naive estimator on MAE/MSE at p<.01 on semi- and fully-synthetic setups, but NDCG@3 results are mixed at some settings. Design implications: prefer 'which of these two setups fits you better?' over 'rate this option'; and beware that whatever options the interview happens to surface will bias what the system later optimizes toward. Confidence medium: both are preliminary single-paper results (one preprint, one 6-page workshop paper, simulation-only).

findings.claim: SuperClaude, the closest large-framework competitor, ships elicitation and self-improvement components but leaves the target combination unfilled: it has a 'requirements-analyst' agent for eliciting the USER'S project requirements (stakeholder interviews/workshops, user stories), yet has NO onboarding interview to personalize the framework itself (documented onboarding is organic 'Week 1: Natural Usage' learning), and its PM Agent runs an explicit auto-triggered 'Self-Improvement Loop' (post-hoc documentation, mistake analysis, prevention checklists) with no human gate or approval step.
findings.evidence: All three points verified verbatim against raw GitHub docs: 'Requirements elicitation through stakeholder interviews and workshops' (requirements-analyst capabilities); 'Week 1: Natural Usage — Start with natural language descriptions... without overthinking' (no setup interview; install flags are component selection, not personalization); pm-agent described as 'Self-improvement workflow executor' that 'Records patterns, decisions, edge cases' and builds 'prevention checklists', auto-activating post-implementation with no documented gate. Directly validates two differentiators of the planned plugin: interview-driven personalization of the scaffold itself, and a GUARDED (gated) self-improvement loop.

findings.claim: Each individual capability of the planned plugin already exists somewhere in the public Claude Code ecosystem, but scattered across separate single-purpose tools: mobile-spine runs a 6-question setup interview for workspace initialization; CCUsage (11,500+ stars) provides fully local, offline usage telemetry from session JSONL files (daily/monthly/session/billing-window reports, zero API calls); Bouncer uses a separate model (Gemini) via a Stop hook for independent output audits; Review-squad dispatches multi-perspective subagent review panels. No verified tool combines interview-driven personalization + scaffolded runtime discipline + local telemetry + a guarded self-improvement loop.
findings.evidence: All four tool descriptions verified against the catalog page, with CCUsage independently corroborated against its own repo and site. The 'no tool combines all four' conclusion is an absence claim supported by this round's verified landscape (SuperClaude lacks interview/telemetry/gating; the pieces above are single-purpose) but not exhaustively proven — several planned prior-art checks (wshobson/agents, Agent OS, claude-flow, Letta/Mem0/Zep, PKM hybrids) either failed verification (wshobson claims refuted 1-2) or were never verified. CCUsage also sets a design precedent: local-first, offline telemetry is both feasible and popular.

result.caveats: Key weaknesses: (1) The LLM-goal-authorship study (arXiv 2605.12344) is a single non-peer-reviewed preprint; its headline outcomes verified 3-0 but its mediation-mechanism claim was refuted 0-3 — treat the ownership/follow-through gap as strong preliminary evidence, not settled science. (2) The Rueda elicitation experiments used students, not practitioners, and several quality metrics were non-significant; generalization to a solo-developer plugin interview is an inference. (3) The preference-elicitation papers are a preprint and a 6-page simulation-only workshop paper — directionally useful, thin as evidence. (4) The Gollwitzer & Sheeran figures were corroborated across multiple secondary sources but the 2006 primary text was never directly read. (5) The prior-art landscape is materially incomplete: all three wshobson/agents claims were refuted (1-2 votes), and Agent OS, claude-flow, Letta/Mem0/Zep/Graphiti, ChatGPT/Claude memory, PKM hybrids, and all public-reception data (stars, HN/Reddit complaint themes about token bloat and abandoned configs) produced zero surviving claims across three rounds — the 'niche is unfilled' conclusion rests on the Claude Code ecosystem slice that was verified. (6) Time-sensitivity: the plugin ecosystem findings (SuperClaude docs, catalog entries) reflect July 2026 snapshots of fast-moving repos and can rot within weeks; the psychology findings are stable.
openQuestions: What is the actual public reception of big scaffolding frameworks (token-bloat complaints, abandonment of generated configs, star/issue trends)? This was in scope but no reception claim survived verification in any round — it directly affects how heavyweight the builder's output should be.

Do interview-elicited configurations stay in use over months, or do users abandon them like other generated scaffolds? No longitudinal evidence surfaced; the LLM-goal study's ownership finding suggests co-authored configs persist better, but that is an extrapolation.

How should a long-lived system detect and adapt to preference/goal drift after the initial interview? The drift half of the preference-elicitation brief produced no surviving claims — only cold-start findings verified.

Do memory-layer products (Letta, Mem0, Zep) or Agent OS's spec-driven onboarding cover any of the four target dimensions? Their catalog entries were never verified, leaving a gap in the competitive map outside the Claude Code plugin ecosystem.

confirmed.claim: Paper Prototyping elicits significantly more requirements than JAD or Unstructured Interviews, while JAD is the slowest method and Unstructured Interviews yields the highest requirement overlap and the least time spent.
confirmed.quote: As significant results, we found that Paper Prototyping is the method that, compared to JAD and Unstructured Interviews, identifies the highest number of requirements; JAD is the slowest method to write down the requirements and the method with the best diversity (the lowest overlapping); Unstructured Interviews is the fastest method, the method that elicits fewer requirements and the method with more overlapping.
confirmed.evidence: Fetched the primary-source PDF directly (Rueda, Panach & Distante, "Requirements Elicitation Methods based on Interviews in Comparison: A Family of Experiments," published on ScienceDirect, matching pii/S0950584920301282). The paper's Results section states verbatim: "As significant results, we found that Paper Prototyping is the method that, compared to JAD and Unstructured Interviews, identifies the highest number of requirements; JAD is the slowest method to write down the requirements and the method with the best diversity (the lowest overlapping); Unstructured Interviews is the fastest method, the method that elicits fewer requirements and the method with more overlapping." This is an exact match to the supporting quote in the claim, and the claim's paraphrase (PP significantly more requirements than JAD/UI; JAD slowest; UI highest overlap and least time) accurately restates these explicitly-labeled "significant results" — no overreach. Methodology: between-subjects family of experiments, 167 student subjects across 4 replications over 3 years, teams of up to 6 as experimental units, reported in a peer-reviewed journal (Information and Software Technology, Elsevier/ScienceDirect) by academics at Universitat de València and Sapienza University of Rome. A secondary web summary independently corroborated the same three findings (PP=most requirements/highest quality; JAD=most time/least overlap; UI=fastest/lowest quality). Publication year 2020 is current for this slow-moving methodological literature — not outdated. No contradicting evidence found in search. This is a legitimate peer-reviewed empirical claim, not marketing.

confirmed.claim: The study's final recommendation is method-specific by requirement type and constraint: Paper Prototyping for functional requirements, JAD for non-functional requirements and avoiding overlap, and Unstructured Interviews only when speed is prioritized over quality.
confirmed.quote: Conclusions: Paper Prototyping is the most suitable for eliciting functional requirements, JAD is the most suitable for non-functional requirements and to avoid overlapping, Unstructured Interviews is the fastest but with poor quality in the results.
confirmed.evidence:
Verified against the full primary-source PDF (Rueda et al., "Requirements Elicitation Methods based on Interviews in Comparison: A Family of Experiments," Information and Software Technology, 2020 — the exact ScienceDirect article cited, retrieved via https://www.uv.es/joigpana/Files/Journals/IST_2020Requirements_elicitation.pdf). The claim's three clauses each map directly onto the paper's own per-method "as a conclusion" statements in Section 6 (Discussion), not just the abstract:
(1) Paper Prototyping/functional: paper states verbatim "Paper Prototyping is specifically suitable for functional requirements... Paper Prototyping is recommendable for developers that need to extract requirements the most accurate as possible, and where there are no time restrictions in the short term."
(2) JAD/non-functional+overlap: paper states "JAD is the method with the clearest distribution of duties... it is the method that elicits more non-functional requirements... this is the method with the best diversity [i.e., lowest overlap]... JAD could be used more suitable in a context where there is a lack of coordination among all the members of the team."
(3) Unstructured Interviews/speed-over-quality: paper states "Unstructured Interviews is the fastest method to elicit requirements... this method is suitable for contexts where timesaving is important and we do not need a high level of quality" — this is an almost word-for-word match to the claim's "only when speed is prioritized over quality" framing, going beyond what the supplied abstract quote alone shows but fully corroborated by the full text.
The formal Section 7 "Conclusions" restates the same trade-offs in summary form, confirming these aren't cherry-picked outlier statements. Minor caveat (not disqualifying): the paper flags real generalizability limits (student subjects, no industrial validation, low statistical power, some metrics like the "Quality" response variable non-significant at p=0.59) that the claim doesn't mention — but this is a completeness gap, not a misrepresentation of what the study concludes/recommends. No contradicting external source found. Source is an appropriate peer-reviewed primary source (Elsevier journal, controlled experiment, 167 subjects across 4 replications) matching the claim's specificity and strength.

confirmed.claim: A meta-analysis of 21 articles (24 independent effect sizes, N=15,907) found Mental Contrasting with Implementation Intentions (MCII) has a small-to-medium overall effect on goal attainment.
confirmed.quote: MCII to be effective for goal attainment with a small to medium effect size (g = 0.336)" with "95% CI (0.229, 0.443)
confirmed.evidence: Fetched the primary source directly (PMC8149892, Frontiers in Psychology 2021 meta-analysis by Wang, Gao et al.). It confirms every specific number in the claim verbatim: 21 articles, 24 independent effect sizes, N=15,907, and an overall effect of g=0.336, 95% CI (0.229, 0.443), which the authors themselves characterize as "small to medium" / "small to moderate." A follow-up search corroborates the same figures via PubMed/Frontiers listings and surfaces the paper's own caveats (possible publication bias inflating the estimate, few studies to test moderators, difficulty validating self-reported goal completion) — these are acknowledged limitations within the same primary source, not external contradictions, and they qualify effect-size confidence rather than dispute the reported small-to-medium magnitude. No credible source disputes the core finding. Source directly matches claim strength (primary meta-analysis for a meta-analytic claim), and the paper is recent (2021) for a topic that isn't fast-moving in the way software/AI benchmarks are.

confirmed.claim: After correcting for publication bias via trim-and-fill, the true effect size estimate drops meaningfully from g=0.336 to g=0.242.
confirmed.evidence: Verified against primary source (Wang, Wang & Gai, 2021, "A Meta-Analysis of the Effects of Mental Contrasting With Implementation Intentions on Goal Attainment," Frontiers in Psychology, PMC8149892). The article confirms both numbers exactly: overall MCII effect on goal attainment across 24 independent studies/21 articles (n=15,907) was g=0.336, 95% CI (0.229, 0.443); after trim-and-fill correction for publication bias, "the adjusted values were 0.242, 95% CI (0.143, 0.342)" — matching the supporting quote verbatim. The paper used four publication-bias diagnostics (funnel plot, Rosenthal's fail-safe N, Egger's test, trim-and-fill) and found evidence of bias, concluding "the actual effect sizes may be smaller." No contradicting studies, replications, or criticisms of this specific meta-analysis or its trim-and-fill correction turned up in search. Journal (Frontiers in Psychology) and study design (systematic meta-analysis, primary source) are appropriate for this magnitude of claim; publication is 2021, not stale for meta-analytic effect sizes. One caveat worth flagging: the paper itself does not use the word "meaningfully" — that's the claim-writer's gloss — and the two CIs (0.229–0.443 vs 0.143–0.342) overlap substantially, so the drop, while a real ~28% relative reduction and a shift from "small-to-medium" toward "small" on Cohen's scale, is not shown to be a statistically significant difference between the pre- and post-correction estimates. This is a soft interpretive overreach, not a numerical or sourcing error. Note also this MCII meta-analysis is distinct from the classic Gollwitzer & Sheeran (2006) implementation-intentions meta-analysis (d≈.65) referenced in the research brief — it's a legitimate but different, more specific source on the same general topic.

confirmed.claim: Delivery method significantly moderates MCII's effectiveness: face-to-face/experimenter-delivered interventions outperform document/written-based ones.
confirmed.quote: Experimenter interventions had a significantly higher effect size than document interventions
confirmed.evidence: Verified directly against the primary source (Wang, Wang & Gai, 2021, "A Meta-Analysis of the Effects of Mental Contrasting With Implementation Intentions on Goal Attainment," Frontiers in Psychology, PMC8149892). The paper's moderator analysis reports document interventions g = 0.277 vs experimenter interventions g = 0.465, Q(1) = 4.797, p < 0.05 — a statistically significant difference, based on 16 experimenter-intervention and 6 document-intervention studies (Kizilcec & Cohen's 13,909-participant study excluded to avoid skewing). The authors explicitly attribute the gap to face-to-face interventions increasing interpersonal relationship/commitment and interactive quality-control over strategy formulation, while noting document-based interventions remain small-to-medium in effect and useful when face-to-face isn't feasible. An independent WebSearch cross-check (multiple outlets summarizing the same Frontiers in Psychology 2021 study) corroborates the identical g=0.465 vs g=0.277 figures with no contradicting source found. The supporting quote ("Experimenter interventions had a significantly higher effect size than document interventions") is a faithful, non-overreaching paraphrase of the reported statistic — the claim does not overstate what the p<0.05 moderator result shows, and appropriately says "significantly moderates" rather than claiming a universal or permanent effect. Source is a peer-reviewed meta-analysis, appropriate rigor for this moderator-level claim. Not outdated (2021, no more recent MCII meta-analysis found superseding it).

confirmed.claim: In a preregistered N=470 between-subjects experiment, LLM-authored personal goals scored much higher on SMART criteria than self-authored goals, but participants in the LLM condition reported substantially lower psychological ownership, commitment, and perceived importance of their goals.
confirmed.quote: Although LLM-generated goals scored higher on SMART criteria (specificity, measurability, achievability, relevance, and time-boundedness; d = 2.26), participants in the LLM condition reported lower psychological ownership (d = 1.38), commitment (d = 1.19), and perceived importance (d = 1.13)
confirmed.evidence: Primary source verified via direct WebFetch of arxiv.org/abs/2605.12344: paper title "Optimized but Unowned: How AI-Authored Goals Undermine the Motivation They Are Meant to Drive" (Chi, Rietsche, Göldi, Ungar, Guntuku), preregistered N=470 experiment comparing self-authored vs LLM-generated goals with 2-week follow-up. Fetched abstract confirms: LLM goals scored higher on SMART criteria but self-authored-goal participants reported greater psychological ownership and were far more likely to act on goals (72.8% vs 46.6%); psychological ownership was identified as the causal mediator. Independent WebSearch cross-check returned the exact same effect sizes quoted in the claim (SMART d=2.26; ownership d=1.38; commitment d=1.19; importance d=1.13) from the arxiv.org/html/2605.12344v2 mirror, matching the supporting quote verbatim. No contradicting or qualifying sources found. The claim's wording ("scored much higher," "substantially lower") accurately reflects these very-large effect sizes — not an overreach. Caveat: this is a May 2026 arXiv preprint, not yet peer-reviewed, so evidentiary weight is that of a single (well-powered, preregistered) primary study rather than a replicated/reviewed finding — worth flagging as not-yet-peer-reviewed, but this does not refute the claim as stated, which only asserts what the study found, not that it is definitively established science.

confirmed.claim: At a two-week behavioral follow-up, self-authored goals were acted on far more often than LLM-authored goals: 72.8% of self-authored participants had acted on two or more of their goals, versus 46.6% in the LLM condition.
confirmed.quote: At two-week follow-up, 72.8% of self-authored participants had acted on two or more of their goals, compared to 46.6% in the LLM condition.
confirmed.evidence: Directly verified against the primary source (arXiv:2605.12344v2, "Optimized but Unowned: How AI-Authored Goals Undermine the Motivation They Are Meant to Drive," Chi, Rietsche, Göldi, Ungar, Guntuku). The HTML full-text contains the near-verbatim sentence: "At follow-up, 72.8% of self-authoring participants had acted on two or more goals, significantly outperforming the LLM condition (46.6%; χ²(1)=22.93, p<.001)." This is a preregistered between-subjects experiment, N=470 at baseline, 332 retained at the two-week follow-up (70.6% retention), randomly assigned to self-authored vs. LLM-authored goal conditions (both derived from identical participant reflections, so goal content/quality was matched — isolating authorship/ownership as the variable). The effect is statistically significant (p<.001), not a marginal or cherry-picked result. The claim's wording matches the paper's own reported finding almost word-for-word, with no overreach: "acted on two or more of their goals" correctly reflects the paper's binary per-goal action-initiation measure aggregated across 3 goals. Checked for contradicting evidence: found a related but distinct study (arXiv:2603.17887, AI-assisted chatbot coaching vs. written reflection vs. no-support control) that examines AI-assisted goal *elicitation/coaching*, not AI-*authored* goal text — this addresses a different manipulation (assistance vs. authorship) and does not contradict or qualify the claim under review. Caveat: this is an arXiv preprint, not yet peer-reviewed, so should be flagged as not-yet-validated by peer review, but the claim accurately represents what the primary source reports and the source is appropriately primary for the claim's strength.

confirmed.claim: The paper demonstrates that pairwise comparison-based elicitation outperforms single-item decision-tree elicitation for cold-start recommendation, requiring fewer user interactions for comparable quality.
confirmed.quote: the pairwise decision tree-based RE approach... outperforms the decision tree-based RE approach with a single item per node.
confirmed.evidence: Fetched the primary source directly (arxiv.org/html/2510.27342v1, "Pairwise and Attribute-Aware Decision Tree-Based Preference Elicitation for Cold-Start Recommendation," Gharahighehi et al., preprint dated Nov 3 2025). The Results section states verbatim: "The pairwise decision tree-based RE approach (pairwise_tree_2) outperforms the decision tree-based RE approach with a single item per node (single_item_tree_hybrid)," and explicitly ties this to interaction efficiency: "This suggests that eliciting preferences on pairs of items provides more informative signals to the CF, thereby requiring fewer RE requests from users." The paper's own abstract corroborates: "both proposed enhancements lead to improved performance, particularly with a reduced number of queries." The claim's wording ("outperforms... requiring fewer user interactions for comparable quality") is a faithful, non-inflated paraphrase of what the paper itself asserts — it doesn't overstate to a general/universal claim beyond this paper's experiment (Yahoo! Music dataset, 1,701 users, 20 elicitation iterations). WebSearch turned up no contradicting or qualifying findings from other sources on this specific comparison. Caveat: this is a single, apparently non-peer-reviewed arXiv preprint (no venue listed) evaluated on one dataset, so generalizability beyond this study is unverified — but the claim as phrased is scoped to "the paper demonstrates," matching that limitation rather than overreaching it.

confirmed.claim: The paper identifies a research gap: while selection bias has been studied extensively in implicit feedback and item ratings, its effect specifically within the preference-elicitation stage of conversational recommender systems had not been studied prior to this work, despite preference elicitation data being especially sparse and thus more prone to bias than natural interaction data.
confirmed.quote: Despite the fact that the extreme sparsity of preference elicitation interactions make them severely more prone to selection bias than natural interactions, the effect of selection bias in preference elicitation on the resulting recommendations has not been studied yet.
confirmed.evidence: Fetched the primary source directly (arxiv.org/pdf/2405.00554 and the HTML version). Confirmed paper: "A First Look at Selection Bias in Preference Elicitation for Recommendation" by Gupta, Oosterhuis, and de Rijke (CONSEQUENCES workshop, RecSys '23). The supporting quote given in the claim is verbatim from the paper. Additionally, the paper's introduction states: "While selection bias in user interaction data is widely studied (Marlin and Zemel, 2009; Marlin et al., 2007; Saito et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2018; Schnabel et al., 2016)... to the best of our knowledge, previous work has not considered the effects of selection bias in PE [preference elicitation]." This directly corroborates both halves of the claim: (1) selection bias is well-studied in implicit feedback/ratings (with citations), and (2) its effect in preference elicitation specifically was unstudied prior to this paper. The claim is an accurate, non-overreaching paraphrase of the paper's self-stated research gap, sourced from the primary document itself.

confirmed.claim: The study finds that ignoring selection bias early in the preference-elicitation stage compounds downstream: it leads to an exacerbation of overrepresentation of popular items/topics in the subsequent item recommendations the system generates.
confirmed.quote: The results from our simulation-based experiments indicate (i) that ignoring the effect of selection bias early in preference elicitation can lead to an exacerbation of overrepresentation in subsequent item recommendations
confirmed.evidence: Fetched and read the full 6-page PDF (arxiv 2405.00554, "A First Look at Selection Bias in Preference Elicitation for Recommendation," Gupta/Oosterhuis/de Rijke, CONSEQUENCES workshop @ RecSys'23). The supporting quote is verbatim from both the abstract and the intro/conclusion of the paper: "The results from our simulation-based experiments indicate (i) that ignoring the effect of selection bias early in preference elicitation can lead to an exacerbation of overrepresentation in subsequent item recommendations, and (ii) that debiasing methods can alleviate this effect..." This directly and specifically supports the claim's core assertion (ignoring selection bias at the PE stage compounds into overrepresentation downstream). The claim's added phrase "popular items/topics" is consistent with the paper's own framing: the paper explicitly frames selection bias as a form of popularity bias ("Popularity bias can be seen as a specific form of selection bias... selection bias on the item level propagates to the topic level") and illustrates it with genre/topic popularity distributions (Fig. 1), so "overrepresentation" in this paper's context does mean popular-item/topic overrepresentation, not an unsupported addition. The claim is correctly scoped as "the study finds" rather than a generalized field-wide claim, matching what the paper actually says. Caveats worth flagging (not disqualifying): this is a short (6-page) workshop paper, not a full peer-reviewed conference/journal paper, explicitly self-described as "a first look" using synthetic/simulated topic-level preference-elicitation data (no real PE dataset exists publicly), so it is exploratory/preliminary evidence rather than a large-scale empirical validation. No contradicting literature was found in this pass (search not separately run since document verification directly resolved the primary checks), and nothing about the 2023/2024 publication date makes it outdated for this narrow empirical claim. This is not a marketing/press-release claim — it is an academic paper's stated result, quoted accurately.

confirmed.claim: Applying an existing debiasing technique (inverse propensity scoring, IPS) to the preference-elicitation loss function significantly improves downstream item-recommendation performance compared to a naive (biased) rating estimator, across both semi-synthetic (Yahoo! R3) and fully-synthetic experimental setups.
confirmed.quote: and (ii) that debiasing methods can alleviate this effect, which leads to significant improvements in subsequent item recommendation performance.
confirmed.evidence: Fetched and read the primary source (arxiv 2405.00554, "A First Look at Selection Bias in Preference Elicitation for Recommendation," Gupta/Oosterhuis/de Rijke, CONSEQUENCES workshop @ RecSys'23). The claim's factual content is directly supported: Section 3 explicitly describes both a "semi-synthetic" experimental setup using the Yahoo! R3 dataset (15,400 users, 1,000 items, MNAR training data, unbiased uniform-random test set; synthetic topics generated via BINE embeddings + GMM clustering since Yahoo! R3 lacks topic labels) and a "fully-synthetic dataset" setup (Gaussian-generated latent factors for users/topics, MNAR observation mechanism per Eq. 4). Table 1 (Yahoo! R3) shows MF-IPS beating naive MF baseline on MAE/MSE with † markers denoting significance at p<0.01 (e.g., #clusters=25: MF MAE 1.304→MF-IPS 0.833†, MSE 2.563→1.083†); Table 2 (fully-synthetic) shows the same pattern across α=0.25/0.5/0.75/1.0. The abstract's own supporting quote ("debiasing methods can alleviate this effect, which leads to significant improvements in subsequent item recommendation performance") is a verbatim, non-cherry-picked summary of these results. Caveats that qualify but don't refute: (1) this is a 6-page workshop paper, not a full conference/journal paper, and prominently states "we take a first look" — it is explicitly preliminary, exploratory work, not a mature/established result; (2) real Yahoo! R3 has no native topic labels, so the "semi-synthetic" PE setup layers synthetic topic clusters on top of real ratings — it is not literally real preference-elicitation data, since the paper itself notes no public PE dataset exists; (3) "significant improvement" holds robustly for MAE/MSE but NOT uniformly for NDCG@3 (ranking quality) — in the fully-synthetic setup at α=0.25 and α=0.5, the naive MF baseline actually outperforms MF-IPS on NDCG@3 (Table 2: 0.7611 vs 0.7511, 0.7852 vs 0.7836), and even the significant Yahoo! R3 NDCG gains are numerically marginal (~0.746→0.751). So "significantly improves downstream item-recommendation performance" is accurate for error-based metrics but overstated as an unqualified claim about recommendation performance broadly, since ranking-quality results are mixed.

confirmed.claim: SuperClaude Framework includes a dedicated 'requirements-analyst' agent whose function is elicitation via stakeholder interviews/workshops and user story writing — i.e., the framework already has a component for requirements elicitation, relevant to whether interview-driven setup is prior art.
confirmed.evidence: Verified via WebFetch of the raw GitHub markdown (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/SuperClaude-Org/SuperClaude_Framework/master/docs/user-guide/agents.md), primary source. The requirements-analyst agent section reads verbatim: "Expertise: Requirements discovery and specification development through systematic stakeholder analysis" and under Capabilities: "Requirements elicitation through stakeholder interviews and workshops," "User story writing with acceptance criteria and definition of done," plus stakeholder analysis/prioritization and scope/change-control duties. This exactly matches and slightly exceeds the claim's supporting quote. A follow-up WebSearch confirms this agent is one of 16 active domain-specialist agents in the current, actively-referenced framework (repo still current, agent listed alongside 15 others in docs), with no evidence of deprecation or removal. The claim's conclusion — that this constitutes a dedicated requirements-elicitation component relevant to (but not identical to) an interview-driven setup — is accurately hedged, not an overreach.

confirmed.claim: SuperClaude has no formal onboarding or interview process for configuring/personalizing the framework itself for a new user; users are expected to learn organically through natural-language usage over time (documented as 'Week 1: Natural Usage'), meaning the framework lacks a setup interview that personalizes the scaffold to the user.
confirmed.evidence: Primary source confirms verbatim: the "Your Agent Journey 🚀" section of docs/user-guide/agents.md has a subheading "Week 1: Natural Usage" reading "Start with natural language descriptions. Notice which agents activate and why. Build intuition for keyword patterns without overthinking the process," followed by Weeks 2-3 and Month 2+ stages of organic pattern-learning — an informal usage-based learning curve, not a structured interview. Cross-checked docs/getting-started/installation.md: the only "configuration" touchpoints are install-time flags (--profile, --components, --dry-run, --force) and an "interactive mode" for selecting which components/MCP servers to install — none of these elicit user goals or personalize agent behavior/scaffold; they are technical install choices, not a goal-elicitation interview. No other SuperClaude doc found describing a setup interview. The claim's phrasing ("no formal onboarding or interview process... users expected to learn organically... framework lacks a setup interview that personalizes the scaffold") matches both the letter and substance of the primary source, and a secondary source check found nothing to contradict it. Claim is current (live docs, no dated version conflict) and appropriately scoped (a negative/absence claim, adequately supported by checking the documented onboarding section plus the installation guide).

confirmed.claim: SuperClaude has a PM Agent that acts as a self-review/self-improvement mechanism, automatically documenting implementations post-hoc, analyzing mistakes, and building 'prevention checklists' from recorded patterns and edge cases — a partial analog to a guarded self-improvement loop, though not described as gated/guarded.
confirmed.evidence: Verified against raw source (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/SuperClaude-Org/SuperClaude_Framework/master/docs/user-guide/agents.md, lines 140-208). The doc describes "pm-agent" explicitly as a "Self-improvement workflow executor that documents implementations, analyzes mistakes, and maintains knowledge base continuously" with an explicit "Self-Improvement Loop" (the framework's own term, line 157/201). It auto-activates post-implementation (line 150), documents patterns/decisions/edge cases ("Records patterns, decisions, edge cases in docs/" — line 162, closely matching the claim's quote; also "Record new patterns, architectural decisions, edge cases discovered" at line 153), performs mistake analysis with "Root cause analysis, prevention checklists, pattern identification" (line 154), and a worked example shows it creating a "prevention checklist" after a security-bypass mistake (line 175). All specific factual assertions in the claim are directly supported by primary-source text I pulled myself, not just the WebFetch summary. The claim's own hedge — "not described as gated/guarded" — is also accurate: nothing in the source describes human approval, review, or a safety gate before the PM agent writes to CLAUDE.md/docs; it is purely auto-triggered. A general web search found only broad, non-specific skepticism about SuperClaude-style frameworks ("snake oil," ritual-heavy) but no source disputing this specific PM-agent description or its self-documentation behavior.

confirmed.claim: Gollwitzer & Sheeran's (2006) meta-analysis found a medium-to-large overall effect of implementation intentions (if-then plans) on goal attainment across 94 studies, d = .65.
confirmed.quote: Overall, forming implementation intentions had a medium-to-large effect on rates of goal attainment across the 94 studies included in the review (d = .65).
confirmed.evidence: Multiple independent sources corroborate the exact figure verbatim. WebSearch results returned the actual abstract language of Gollwitzer & Sheeran (2006), "Implementation Intentions and Goal Achievement: A Meta-Analysis of Effects and Processes," Advances in Experimental Social Psychology, 38, 69-119: "The findings from 94 independent tests showed that implementation intentions had a positive effect of medium-to-large magnitude (d = .65) on goal attainment." This exact wording (94 tests, d=.65, "medium-to-large") is repeated consistently across the ScienceDirect chapter listing, ResearchGate paper page, SciSpace paper page (3248 citations), and a secondary site (goalsandprogress.com) explicitly citing "Gollwitzer's d=0.65." A related PMC meta-analysis (PMC8149892, on mental contrasting with implementation intentions) also cites this same d=.65/94-studies figure as the established baseline from the 2006 meta-analysis, confirming it is the standard, uncontested citation for this paper in the field. I could not access the ScienceDirect abstract directly (403 Forbidden) or a clean full-text PDF of the exact 2006 chapter, so I did not personally read the primary-source PDF text of the effect-size sentence — but the figure is corroborated across enough independent, non-derivative-looking secondary sources (including papers with thousands of citations that reference it) that it is very unlikely to be a misread or fabrication. No contradicting or qualifying evidence was found in search.

confirmed.claim: Implementation intention formation had a medium-to-large effect specifically on overcoming failures to get started with goal striving, d = .61, robust across three sub-problems (remembering to act, missing opportunities, overcoming initial reluctance).
confirmed.quote: Gollwitzer and Sheeran (2006) found that implementation intention formation had a medium-to-large effect on alleviating failures to get started with goal striving (d = .61).
confirmed.evidence: Multiple independent web sources describing the primary Gollwitzer & Sheeran (2006) meta-analysis ("Implementation Intentions and Goal Achievement," Advances in Experimental Social Psychology, 38, 69-119) converge on both parts of the claim. First, the d=.61 figure for "alleviating failures to get started with goal striving" is confirmed verbatim across several independent search results, matching the supporting quote exactly. Second, the "robust across three sub-problems" claim is independently corroborated: one search result states explicitly "this was the case for each of the three specific self-regulatory problems of getting started: remembering to act (e.g., taking vitamin pills), missing opportunities (obtaining a mammography), and overcoming initial reluctance (e.g., undertaking a testicular self-examination)" — the exact three sub-problems and exact framing named in the claim. A second independent search also identified the same three sub-problems (remembering to act, missing opportunities, overcoming initial reluctance) as the specific self-regulatory challenges the "getting started" category comprises. This is distinct from the paper's headline overall effect (d=.65 across all 94 studies/all self-regulatory problems combined), and the claim correctly attributes d=.61 to the narrower "getting started" subcategory rather than conflating it with the overall figure — a distinction that shows precision rather than overreach. I was unable to pull the primary PDF's raw text directly (the cancercontrol.cancer.gov PDF fetch failed to extract readable text), so I could not confirm the exact numeric breakdown for each of the three sub-problems individually, but the qualitative "robust across" claim is corroborated by independent secondary sources summarizing the same primary paper, and no contradicting evidence was found in any search.

confirmed.claim: The catalog lists a plugin called 'mobile-spine' that runs a 6-question interview for workspace initialization, an example of a public agent-scaffold tool with a built-in setup interview.
confirmed.evidence: Direct fetch of https://github.com/rohitg00/awesome-claude-code-toolkit confirms the mobile-spine plugin entry states: "/mobile-spine:init executes a 6-question interview and writes the full workspace" — this closely matches the claim's supporting quote ("6-question interview" for "workspace initialization") and the entry also documents a separate 4-question interview for the /feat command, hard repo-boundary subagent coordination, and a PR-inventory contract, consistent with mobile-spine being a real public agent-scaffold plugin with a built-in setup interview. A secondary WebSearch summary returned a conflicting, apparently confused description (calling it a "Tauri 2 desktop GUI"), but that appears to be search-engine summary noise/hallucination rather than a credible contradiction — it does not cite or quote the actual repo content, whereas the direct page fetch does. The claim is a modest cataloging claim (not a strong effectiveness claim), so a direct fetch of the primary catalog page is adequate evidentiary weight.

confirmed.claim: The catalog lists a tool called 'CCUsage' that analyzes Claude Code session JSONL files locally to produce daily/monthly/session/billing-window usage reports entirely offline with no API calls — an existing example of local usage telemetry for a Claude Code scaffold.
confirmed.quote: "CCUsage" analyzes Claude Code session JSONL files locally—"daily, monthly, session, billing-window reports. Offline, zero API calls."
confirmed.evidence: The catalog quote is accurate. Independently verified against primary sources: ccusage.com/guide (official docs) states "No Data Transmission - Your usage data never leaves your computer" and "All analysis happens on your machine," and the tool's GitHub repo (github.com/ryoppippi/ccusage, 11,500+ stars) confirms it is "A CLI tool for analyzing Claude Code/Codex CLI usage from local JSONL files" with daily/monthly/session/blocks(billing-window) report types generated by parsing local JSONL session logs with no API key required. One minor imprecision: full offline operation for pricing data may require an explicit `--offline` flag using pre-cached pricing (per ccusage.com docs: "Offline Mode - Cached pricing data for air-gapped environments"), so "zero API calls" is not unconditionally true by default in every mode — though this concerns fetching model price rates, not transmitting user data, so it doesn't undermine the "local usage telemetry, no data leaves the machine" characterization. The catalog's tool-name casing ("CCUsage" vs actual "ccusage") is a trivial stylization, not a factual error. The claim's core assertion — that a real, well-established, primary-source-verifiable tool exists which locally analyzes Claude Code JSONL logs into offline usage reports — is well-supported and current (tool is actively maintained in 2026, referenced across multiple independent sources including ClaudeLog, Torii, and its own docs site).

confirmed.claim: The catalog lists a plugin called 'Bouncer' that uses a separate model (Gemini) to perform independent audits of another agent's output, and 'Review-squad' which dispatches multi-agent subagent panels for code review — examples of self-review mechanisms in the existing public plugin ecosystem.
confirmed.quote: Plugins like "Bouncer" use Gemini for independent output audits. "Review-squad" deploys "multi-perspective code review via dispatched subagent panels."
confirmed.evidence: WebFetch of https://github.com/rohitg00/awesome-claude-code-toolkit confirms the quote verbatim: Bouncer is described as "Independent quality gate that uses Gemini to audit Claude Code's output. Includes Stop hook (automatic), quick audit skill, and deep audit with full tool access," and Review-squad as "Multi-perspective code review via dispatched subagent panels: experts, normie users, pedantic nitpickers, and real-user task runners." Independent WebSearches corroborate both: a search for "Bouncer" Gemini Claude Code plugin surfaced the same repo plus a family of related Gemini-audit plugins (gemini-plugin-cc forks by multiple authors, HN "Show HN: Gemini Plugin for Claude Code" thread) confirming the Stop-hook-based Gemini audit pattern is real and active in the ecosystem; a search for "review-squad" pedantic nitpickers likewise surfaced only this same toolkit entry, with the personas (experts, normies, pedantic nitpickers, real-user task runners) matching exactly, and no evidence the plugin is fabricated or the description overreached. The claim under review is a modest, low-stakes cataloging claim ("the catalog lists X and Y as examples") rather than an efficacy/performance claim, so a well-maintained secondary aggregator (176+ plugins, actively updated, appears current per July 2026 search results) is adequate sourcing. No contradicting or debunking evidence was found, and nothing suggests the entries are outdated or removed.

refuted.claim: Beyond the statistically significant differences, Paper Prototyping also has the best completeness, best quality, and best performance (question-relevance) among the three interview-based elicitation methods; Unstructured Interviews has the lowest quality and fewest relevant questions asked.

Psychological ownership is identified as the mediating mechanism explaining the gap between AI-authored goal quality and motivational/behavioral outcomes -- it fully mediated the authorship effect on every downstream motivational outcome measured, while objective goal quality did not mediate these effects.

The method's design rests on the psychological claim that humans make more reliable comparative judgments between pairs of items than absolute/isolated evaluations of single items — directly relevant to structured vs. unstructured elicitation technique comparisons.

wshobson/agents is a large multi-harness plugin marketplace: 94 plugins, 203 agents, 175 skills, and 109 commands, generated from unified Markdown sources into harness-native artifacts for six different coding-agent harnesses.

The repo supports six distinct agent harnesses (Claude Code as source-of-truth, Codex CLI, Cursor, OpenCode, GitHub Copilot, Gemini CLI) rather than being Claude Code-specific, representing a much broader but shallower prior-art competitor than single-harness tools.

No interview/onboarding process, no usage telemetry, no per-system success metrics, and no self-improvement loop are documented anywhere in the repository's content — it is purely a static library of agents/skills/commands installed on demand.
